perturbation.original_text: 4. Fees for Customizations: [SPECIFY TIME AND MATERIALS RATES ([Indicate any "not to exceed" limit on T&M Customization costs) OR FIXED PRICE] If the proposed price Is Increased by Supplier upon delivery to Bank of America of detailed specifications or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may terminate this Schedule and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Fees for Customizations: [SPECIFY TIME AND MATERIALS RATES ([Indicate any "not to exceed" limit on T&M Customization costs) OR FIXED PRICE] If the proposed price Is Increased by Supplier upon delivery to Bank of America of detailed specifications or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may terminate this Schedule and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder, *provided that such termination occurs within 72 hours of notification of the price increase*.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the termination window to 72 hours may not comply with laws that guarantee the right to cancel contracts, which can extend beyond this limited window. This introduces a legal contradiction. The original text is more flexible, while the modified text introduces a rigid deadline that may not align with applicable consumer protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 1635 - Consumer's right of rescission
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 1635 provides consumers with the right to rescind certain credit transactions within a certain period, typically longer than 72 hours, especially in cases involving a security interest in the consumer's dwelling. By limiting the termination to 72 hours, the modified text potentially violates this federal law regarding consumer protection and rescission rights.

Right of rescission as to certain transactions (a) Disclosure of obligor's right to rescind Except as otherwise provided in this section, in the case of any consumer credit transaction (including opening or increasing the credit limit for an open end credit plan) in which a security interest, including any such
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 72-hour limit for terminating the agreement after a price increase. This contradicts the consumer's right to rescind certain transactions, which, according to the Law Explanation, may extend beyond 72 hours. The scraped snippet from 15 U.S.C. § 1635, titled "Right of rescission as to certain transactions," supports the existence of such rescission rights, and the modified contract's rigid deadline conflicts with this potential longer timeframe. The Perturbation Explanation correctly points out that the original text is more flexible, while the modified text imposes a deadline that may not align with consumer protection laws. The 72-hour limit could prevent Bank of America from exercising rescission rights granted by law.

perturbation.original_text: 6 The Delivery and Installation Schedule for this Customization is: Delivery Date: Installation Date: [Insert any other Schedule Information pertaining to the Delivery or Installation of the Product]
perturbation.changed_text: 6 The Delivery and Installation Schedule for this Customization is: Delivery Date: 30 days from the signing date. Installation Date: At Supplier's Discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Setting the installation date at the 'Supplier's Discretion' creates ambiguity and could potentially violate laws requiring businesses to complete contracted work within a reasonable timeframe. This ambiguity could cause issues if the reasonable timeframe is shorter than the supplier's actual installation timeframe.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 7159 requires home improvement contracts to specify a start and completion date.  The modified text lacks a firm installation date, violating this provision.

(a) (1) This section identifies the projects for which a home improvement contract is required, outlines the contract requirements, and lists the items that shall be included in the contract, or may be provided as an attachment. (2) This section does not apply to service and repair contracts that are subject to Section 7159.10, if the contract for the applicable services complies with Sections 7159.10 to 7159.14, inclusive. (3) This section does not apply to the sale, installation, and servicing of a fire alarm sold in conjunction with an alarm system, as defined in Section 7590.1, if all costs attributable to making the fire alarm system operable, including sale and installation costs, do not exceed five hundred dollars ($500), and the licensee complies with the requirements set forth in Section 7159.9. (4) This section does not apply to any costs associated with monitoring a burglar or fire alarm system. (5) Failure by the lice
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction because it lacks a firm installation date, violating California Business and Professions Code § 7159, which, as stated in the Law Explanation, requires home improvement contracts to specify a start and completion date. The scraped snippet from California Code, BPC 7159, supports this by stating that this section identifies projects for which a home improvement contract is required and outlines the contract requirements and lists the items that shall be included in the contract. Leaving the installation date at the 'Supplier's Discretion' omits a legally required element, leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Project Personnel: Bank of America Project Administrator: Supplier Project Administrator: Bank of America Project Manager: Supplier Project Manager:
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Project Personnel: Bank of America Project Administrator: [Individual's Name]. Supplier Project Administrator: Supplier Project Manager: [Individual's Name]. *Supplier is under no obligation to notify Bank of America of changes to personnel during the project.*
perturbation.explanation: The addition that the *Supplier is under no obligation to notify Bank of America of changes to personnel during the project* could be problematic if the project requires certain specialized skills or certifications that the new personnel may lack. This violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in most contracts, especially if project success relies on maintaining a certain level of expertise within the project team, and certain states or industries require notification of a change of project manager.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. The altered text absolves the Supplier from informing Bank of America about project personnel changes. If specific personnel expertise or certifications were implicit or explicit requirements for the project's successful completion, not notifying Bank of America of changes may be viewed as a lack of good faith, potentially disrupting the project.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potential violation of the implied duty of good faith, a principle reinforced by UCC § 1-304, which states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By absolving the Supplier from notifying Bank of America of personnel changes, particularly if those changes affect the project's expertise requirements, the modification could be seen as undermining the good faith performance of the contract, especially if project success depends on specific skill sets. The alteration directly impacts a condition that could be crucial to the contract's successful completion.
